MILO_OFFICIAL_SCRIPT_v1

seed_id: seed-103
edit_level: rewrite
script:
  setup: "My Tinder profile says I have a corner office with city views and drive a six-figure vehicle."
  punchline: "I'm a bus driver."
  tag: ~
changes_made: Compressed significantly. Original setup ran three sentences including 'divorced dad' framing and two separate claim lines. Cut 'divorced dad' — the self-deprecating context it adds isn't worth the runtime cost, and the misdirection works cleaner without it. Merged the two Tinder claims into one compound sentence so the false bragging reads as a single burst rather than two beats. Changed '$100,000 vehicle' to 'six-figure vehicle' — flows better in speech, sounds more like how someone would actually write a Tinder bio. Punchline is untouched: 'I'm a bus driver.' is already perfect. Three words, complete misdirection payoff.
caption_notes: Two cards. Setup: "My Tinder profile says I have a corner office with city views and drive a six-figure vehicle." Punchline: "I'm a bus driver." Consider holding punchline card slightly longer — the viewer needs a beat to do the math (corner office = driver's cab, city views = windshield, six-figure vehicle = the bus).
timing_notes: Setup delivered with mild pride — this person genuinely believes their Tinder profile is doing something. 0.5s pause. Punchline flat, matter-of-fact. The humor is the gap between what he claims and what's true; the delivery should not wink at the audience.
tts_notes: 'six-figure' — two words, TTS will handle correctly.
quality_flag: strong

IRIS_VIDEO_PROMPT_v1

seed_id: seed-103
concepts_count: 3
casting_note: No RILEY_CASTING_PLAN provided; Iris assigned casting inline.

Cast assignments:
1. Jerry Gold — Bus driver / Tinder narrator (Classic Dad persona). Delivers the setup with genuine pride. The punchline is not self-deprecating to HIM — he genuinely believes his profile is accurate.
2. Zoe Sparks — Tinder date (Overexcited Rep persona). Appears in date reveal concepts. Her initial excitement crumbling into confusion is the reaction shot.

---

## Concept A — "The Profile vs Reality"
Style: Split visual — glamorous Tinder profile imagery that cuts to mundane bus driver truth
Duration: 9.6s — Needs time for the misdirection setup to build before the reveal.

Master video prompt:
"Two contrasting visual modes in one vertical frame (9:16), cartoon 2.5D illustration, clean bold lines. FIRST HALF: Luxurious imagery — a corner office with floor-to-ceiling windows showing a city skyline, a sleek expensive car. SECOND HALF: The mundane truth — a city bus interior, driver's seat, the same skyline visible through the big windshield. Jerry Gold (brown hair, mustache) appears in both — first in a suit looking successful, then in a bus driver uniform looking exactly the same level of proud. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Tinder Profile
A smartphone screen fills the vertical frame, showing a dating profile. At the top, a profile photo of Jerry Gold — a middle-aged man with brown hair, handlebar mustache, wearing a sharp teal blazer over a white dress shirt, photographed from a flattering angle. Below the photo: "Jerry, 47" and a bio section. The profile background is warm and polished. Heart and X buttons visible at the bottom. The phone screen is centered in frame, slightly tilted, as if someone is swiping through the app. Warm rosy lighting like a dating app's color palette. Everything looks impressive.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.5s): SETUP — The Claims
The Tinder profile has expanded. Now the viewer sees Jerry Gold's profile in detail — same flattering photo (brown hair, mustache, teal blazer). Next to or below the photo: visual representations of his claims. A small illustration of a corner office with floor-to-ceiling windows and a panoramic city skyline. Below that, a silhouette of a sleek luxury vehicle. The overall feel is aspirational — warm filters, success imagery. Jerry's profile photo shows him looking off into the distance with quiet confidence, the way people do in dating profiles when they want to seem thoughtful. The phone interface frames everything — this is clearly a dating app.
Caption: "My Tinder profile says I have a corner office with city views and drive a six-figure vehicle."

Scene 3 (5.5–6.0s): THE PAUSE
Same phone screen with Jerry's dating profile visible. A moment of stillness. Then the phone screen begins to crack or glitch — a visual hint that reality is about to intrude. The warm rosy filter flickers. Something is about to change.

Scene 4 (6.0–9.6s): PUNCHLINE — The Truth
The phone/app interface has dissolved. Now the viewer sees the reality: the interior of a city bus, viewed from behind the driver's seat. Jerry Gold sits in the driver's seat — same brown hair, same handlebar mustache, but now wearing a blue bus driver uniform with a name tag and a hat. He sits with the exact same posture and expression of quiet pride from his profile photo. Through the bus's enormous front windshield: a panoramic city skyline — his "city views." The driver's cab IS his "corner office" — it's in the front corner of the bus. The bus itself is the "six-figure vehicle" (buses cost $300K+). Everything in his profile was technically true. The bus interior is utilitarian — plastic seats, handrails, a fare box. Jerry looks directly at the viewer with complete sincerity. He sees no deception. He is a man with a corner office and city views.
Caption: "I'm a bus driver."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.5s setup | 6.0–9.6s punchline
Voice: Jerry with mild pride through setup — he genuinely believes his profile is good. 0.5s pause. Punchline delivered matter-of-fact, as a clarification not a confession. No wink, no shame.
Risk: LOW. Bus interior is a recognizable setting. The Tinder UI elements may render imperfectly but the phone framing helps. Fallback: skip the phone interface, just show Jerry in a fancy office (setup) then hard cut to Jerry in bus driver seat (punchline).
Jerry hook: "The visual misdirection IS the joke — the profile images are technically true, which makes the punchline land harder. Plus Jerry in a bus driver uniform with the same proud expression is inherently funny."
Vince: 9:16, 9.6s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 bus driver reveal, Scene 2 profile claims], trims=[Scene 3 can cut to 0.3s, Scene 1 can shorten to 1.5s]

## Concept B — "The Date Reveal"
Style: First date at a restaurant, the moment she asks what he does
Duration: 9.4s — Tight two-person scene with reveal.

Master video prompt:
"Restaurant booth, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Jerry Gold (brown hair, mustache, teal jacket or dress shirt) and Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, nice date outfit) seated across from each other at a small restaurant table with candles and menus. Warm romantic lighting. Jerry looks confident. Zoe looks excited. Caption-safe lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.0s): HOOK — The Date
A cozy restaurant booth with warm amber lighting from small table candles. A round table with a white tablecloth, two menus, two water glasses, a small vase with a flower. Across from each other sit Jerry Gold (middle-aged man, brown hair, handlebar mustache, wearing a teal jacket over a dress shirt — date night outfit) and Zoe Sparks (young woman, orange-magenta two-tone hair, teal glasses, hoop earrings, wearing a nice blouse — date night look). Jerry leans back slightly, one arm draped over the booth back, radiating confidence. Zoe leans forward, chin on her hand, eyes bright behind her glasses — she's charmed. A first date going well. The restaurant is dimly lit, romantic, upscale-ish.

Scene 2 (2.0–5.5s): SETUP — The Brag
Same restaurant booth. Jerry Gold (brown hair, mustache, teal jacket, date outfit) is mid-sentence, gesturing casually with one hand while the other rests on the table. He's describing his work situation with quiet pride — not bragging, just... stating facts about his impressive life. Zoe Sparks across the table (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses, date blouse) listens with wide-eyed interest, one hand touching her earring. She is IMPRESSED. The candlelight flickers between them. The menus have been pushed aside — they're past ordering, deep in conversation. Jerry looks like a man who believes every word he's saying.
Caption: "My Tinder profile says I have a corner office with city views and drive a six-figure vehicle."

Scene 3 (5.5–6.0s): THE BEAT
Same restaurant. Closer framing on Jerry Gold's face across the candlelit table. He pauses. A slight, satisfied smile. He's about to tell her what he does. It's his favorite part of the date. He considers it the closer.

Scene 4 (6.0–9.4s): PUNCHLINE — The Reveal
Same restaurant booth. Jerry Gold (brown hair, mustache, teal jacket) sits with complete composure, hands folded on the table, slight proud nod. He has said it. He waits for her admiration. Across the table, Zoe Sparks (orange-magenta hair, teal glasses) has frozen mid-sip of her water glass — the glass hovers near her mouth, her eyes have gone wide behind her teal frames, her expression is a perfect mixture of confusion, recalculation, and dawning realization. She is doing the math. Corner office... city views... six-figure vehicle... bus. The candle between them flickers. Jerry does not read her expression correctly — he interprets her shock as being impressed.
Caption: "I'm a bus driver."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.0s no text | 2.0–5.5s setup | 6.0–9.4s punchline
Voice: Jerry with warm confidence, like a man on a good date. 0.5s pause. Punchline matter-of-fact and proud. No self-awareness.
Risk: LOW. Restaurant scene is straightforward. Fallback: simplify to single-shot Jerry at a table talking to camera.
Jerry hook: "Zoe's frozen-mid-sip reaction shot is a meme waiting to happen. The date setting adds stakes the profile-only version doesn't have."
Vince: 9:16, 9.4s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline+Zoe frozen reaction, Scene 2 bragging], trims=[Scene 3 cut to 0.3s]

## Concept C [SURREAL] — "The Transforming Office"
Style: Jerry's "corner office" physically morphs from corporate to bus in real-time
Duration: 10.0s — Needs time for the transformation.

surreal_element: Jerry sits in what appears to be a luxury corner office. As he delivers the punchline, the office transforms around him — the desk becomes a steering wheel, the floor-to-ceiling windows become the bus windshield, the leather chair becomes the driver's seat, the skyline starts MOVING because the building was always a bus. Jerry doesn't react to any of it.
Weirdness budget: 5/10 — The transformation is smooth and logical, not chaotic. It's a visual pun, not a nightmare.

Master video prompt:
"A corner office that transforms into a bus driver's cab, cartoon 2.5D, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16. Jerry Gold (brown hair, mustache) sits at a desk that becomes a steering wheel. Floor-to-ceiling windows that become a bus windshield. The city skyline through the windows begins to MOVE — the building was a bus. Jerry's suit transitions to a bus driver uniform. He notices nothing."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.5s): HOOK — The Corner Office
A luxurious corner office high in a skyscraper. Floor-to-ceiling windows wrap around two walls, showing a stunning city skyline — glass buildings, blue sky, tiny cars below. Jerry Gold sits at a large mahogany desk in a high-backed leather chair — a middle-aged man with brown hair, handlebar mustache, wearing a sharp dark suit and teal tie. His hands rest on the desk. A laptop, a desk phone, a coffee mug, and a small framed photo sit on the desk. The office is immaculate — potted plant in the corner, certificates on the wall. Jerry looks directly at the viewer with quiet pride. This is a man who has made it.

Scene 2 (2.5–5.5s): SETUP — The Claims
Same corner office. Jerry Gold at his mahogany desk (brown hair, mustache, dark suit, teal tie) gestures toward the window view with one hand — presenting his city views. The skyline glitters behind him. His leather chair is plush. The desk is polished. But something is slightly wrong — the viewer might notice that the desk has a strange curved edge on one side. The certificates on the wall are slightly blurry. The coffee mug has an odd shape. These are subtle hints. Jerry doesn't notice any of them. He is presenting his life with complete sincerity.
Caption: "My Tinder profile says I have a corner office with city views and drive a six-figure vehicle."

Scene 3 (5.5–7.0s): TRANSFORMATION BEAT
The office begins to change. The mahogany desk stretches and curves — its surface becoming a dashboard, a steering wheel emerging from its center. The leather chair flattens and its armrests fold into the sides of a bus driver's seat. The floor-to-ceiling windows remain but their frames become the metal frame of a bus windshield. The city skyline through the windows BEGINS TO MOVE — buildings sliding laterally as if the entire office is in motion. The potted plant in the corner becomes a fare box. The certificates on the wall become route maps. Jerry Gold sits in the middle of all this, expression unchanged, hands now on the steering wheel that was his desk. His dark suit has become a blue bus driver's uniform with a name tag. He has not reacted to any of this. The transformation is smooth, dream-like, and completely ignored by its only witness.

Scene 4 (7.0–10.0s): PUNCHLINE — The Bus
The transformation is complete. Jerry Gold sits in a city bus driver's seat — blue uniform, driver's cap, name tag, hands on the steering wheel. Through the large bus windshield: the same city skyline, but now it's moving — buildings slide past as the bus drives through city streets. Tiny pedestrians are visible on the sidewalks below/outside. The bus interior stretches behind him — plastic seats, handrails, a digital route display reading "LINE 47." In the rearview mirror above Jerry's head, blurred passengers are visible. Jerry looks at the viewer with the exact same proud expression from Scene 1. Nothing has changed for him. He has always been here. The corner office was always a bus. The desk was always a steering wheel. The city views were always the windshield. He is a man who told the truth.
Caption: "I'm a bus driver."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.5s no text | 2.5–5.5s setup | 7.0–10.0s punchline
Transformation beat: 5.5–7.0s — office furniture morphs into bus components, skyline starts moving, suit becomes uniform.
Voice: Same as other concepts. Proud, matter-of-fact. 0.5s pause. Punchline sincere. He told the truth and he knows it.
Risk: MEDIUM. The morphing transformation is complex for AI gen. Fallback: hard cut from corner office (freeze frame) to bus interior — skip the morph, let the visual contrast carry it.
Jerry hook: "This one's weird on purpose. The AI will make it weirder. That's the bit. But the concept is actually elegant — everything in his profile was technically true, and the transformation proves it visually. The skyline MOVING is the moment the audience gets it."
Vince: 9:16, 10.0s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 bus reveal, Scene 3 transformation, Scene 1 office for contrast], trims=[Scene 1 can shorten to 2.0s]
